content:
# Romans Chapter 9 — Disagreement Log
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Date:** 2026-03-05

This log records all verses in Romans Chapter 9 where the three translators (EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA) significantly diverged in their renderings, along with SOPHIA's resolution and reasoning.

---

## 9:1 — "Truth I speak" vs. "I speak the truth" vs. "With Christ as my witness, I speak with utter truthfulness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Truth I speak in Christos, I do not lie, the conscience of me bearing witness with me in [the] Holy Spirit" | Preserves Greek word order (Ἀλήθειαν λέγω) with fronted object for emphasis; retains "Christos" untranslated |
| LOGOS | "I speak the truth in Christ — I am not lying; my conscience bears me witness in the Holy Spirit" | Natural English word order while preserving formal register |
| PNEUMA | "With Christ as my witness, I speak with utter truthfulness. My conscience and the Holy Spirit confirm it." | Restructures into two sentences; adds "utter" for emphasis; interprets "in Christ" as "with Christ as my witness" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, which preserves the emphasis of the Greek without awkward inversion or over-interpretation. EMET's fronted word order is too wooden; PNEUMA's "with Christ as my witness" overinterprets "in Christ" (ἐν Χριστῷ).

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. SOPHIA's text reads: "I speak the truth in Christ — I am not lying; my conscience bears me witness in the Holy Spirit —"

---

## 9:3 — "accursed" and scope of Paul's anguish

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For I was wishing I myself to be accursed from the Christos for the sake of the brothers of me, the kinsmen of me according to [the] flesh" | Retains imperfect tense "was wishing"; literal possessives; "accursed from the Christos" |
| LOGOS | "For I could wish that I myself were accursed and cut off from Christ for the sake of my brothers, my kinsmen according to the flesh" | Renders imperfect as "could wish" (potentiality); adds "cut off from" for clarity |
| PNEUMA | "I would be willing to be forever cursed — cut off from Christ! — if that would save them." | Adds "forever," em-dash interjection, and conditional "if that would save them" (not in Greek); adds "brothers and sisters" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "I could wish" for the imperfect tense, and "accursed and cut off from Christ" for ἀνάθεμα εἶναι αὐτὸς ἐγὼ ἀπὸ τοῦ Χριστοῦ. PNEUMA's additions ("forever," "if that would save them") are interpretive expansions not warranted by the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS with minor EMET input. SOPHIA's text follows LOGOS.

---

## 9:4 — Structure and emphasis of Israel's privileges

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "who are Israelites, whose [is] the adoption and the glory and the covenants and the giving of the law and the worship and the promises" | Preserves relative clause structure; uses "and" between every item |
| LOGOS | "They are Israelites, and to them belong the adoption, the glory, the covenants, the giving of the law, the worship, and the promises." | Clean English list with serial comma |
| PNEUMA | "They are the people of Israel, chosen to be God's adopted children. God revealed his glory to them. He made covenants with them and gave them his law. He gave them the privilege of worshiping him and receiving his wonderful promises." | Breaks single sentence into multiple; adds "chosen," "privilege," "wonderful" — interpretive expansions |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's expansion ("chosen to be," "privilege of," "wonderful") adds theological weight not present in the Greek, which simply lists the privileges with definite articles.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:5 — Christological doxology: "God over all, blessed forever"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "whose [are] the fathers, and from whom [is] the Christos according to [the] flesh, the [one] being over all, God blessed into the ages. Amen." | Translates participially; preserves punctuation ambiguity somewhat; "God blessed into the ages" |
| LOGOS | "To them belong the patriarchs, and from their race, according to the flesh, is the Christ, who is God over all, blessed forever. Amen." | Resolves punctuation in favor of Christ being called "God over all" |
| PNEUMA | "And he is God, the one who rules over everything and is worthy of eternal praise! Amen." | Emphatically applies doxology to Christ; adds "rules over" and "worthy of" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, which grammatically resolves the doxology as applying to Christ — the most natural reading of the Greek. EMET's word order preserves more ambiguity. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the elegance of the doxological form.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The grammar most naturally reads the doxology as applying to Christ.

---

## 9:7 — "Through Isaac shall your offspring be named"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "In Isaak shall seed be called to you" | Hyper-literal; "seed" singular; "in" rather than "through" |
| LOGOS | "Through Isaac shall your offspring be named" | Formal but readable; "offspring" for σπέρμα |
| PNEUMA | "Isaac is the son through whom your descendants will be counted" | Interprets the quotation; pluralizes "descendants"; adds "son" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "In Isaak shall seed be called to you" is too opaque for English readers. PNEUMA's interpretation is accurate but moves too far from the quotation form.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:12 — "The older will serve the younger" vs. "The greater will serve the lesser"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "The greater will serve the lesser" | Translates μείζων/ἐλάσσων as comparative adjectives of degree (greater/lesser) |
| LOGOS | "The older will serve the younger" | Translates as age-related (standard English rendering of the OT quotation) |
| PNEUMA | "Your older son will serve your younger son" | Adds "your" and "son" for narrative clarity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "older/younger" which follows the Genesis 25:23 context and is the standard English rendering. EMET's "greater/lesser" is a valid translation of the Greek but loses the birth-order context. PNEUMA's additions are unnecessary.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:13 — "Esau I hated" vs. "I rejected Esau"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Iakob I loved, but Esau I hated." | Retains "hated" (ἐμίσησα); preserves Hebrew idiom directly |
| LOGOS | "Jacob I loved, but Esau I hated." | Same as EMET but with anglicized names |
| PNEUMA | "I loved Jacob, but I rejected Esau." | Changes "hated" to "rejected" — an interpretive rendering of the Semitic idiom |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, retaining "hated" as the translation of ἐμίσησα. While PNEUMA's "rejected" captures one dimension of the Semitic comparative idiom, "hated" is the actual word used and its jarring quality prompts necessary theological reflection.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS/EMET. The word "hated" is retained.

---

## 9:14 — "Injustice on God's part?" vs. "Unrighteousness with the God?"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "[Is there] unrighteousness with the God? May it never be!" | "Unrighteousness" (ἀδικία); "May it never be" (μὴ γένοιτο) |
| LOGOS | "Is there injustice on God's part? By no means!" | "Injustice" for ἀδικία; "By no means" for μὴ γένοιτο |
| PNEUMA | "Are we saying, then, that God was unfair? Of course not!" | "Unfair" for ἀδικία; "Of course not" for μὴ γένοιτο |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Injustice" is more precise than PNEUMA's "unfair" (which is colloquial) and more readable than EMET's "unrighteousness." "By no means" captures the emphatic force of μὴ γένοιτο better than "Of course not."

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:16 — "human will or exertion" vs. "the one willing nor the one running"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not of the [one] willing nor of the [one] running, but of the God having mercy" | Retains participial forms; "running" for τρέχοντος |
| LOGOS | "it depends not on human will or exertion, but on God, who has mercy" | Abstracts the participles into nouns; "exertion" for τρέχοντος |
| PNEUMA | "So it is God who decides to show mercy. We can neither choose it nor work for it." | Complete paraphrase; replaces metaphor with explanation |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "running" is a more literal translation of τρέχοντος but "exertion" captures the metaphorical sense. PNEUMA's paraphrase loses the proverbial quality of the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:20 — "O man" vs. "O human" vs. "a mere human being"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "O human, but who are you, the [one] answering back to the God?" | Uses "human" for ἄνθρωπε; retains vocative |
| LOGOS | "But who are you, O man, to answer back to God?" | Traditional "O man"; formal address |
| PNEUMA | "No, don't say that. Who are you, a mere human being, to argue with God?" | Adds "No, don't say that"; "mere human being" for ἄνθρωπε; "argue" for ἀνταποκρινόμενος |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering with its formal vocative "O man." EMET's "O human" is gender-inclusive but less traditional. PNEUMA adds "No, don't say that" which is not in the text, and "mere" is an addition.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:21 — "right" vs. "authority" over the clay

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Or does not the potter have authority over the clay" | "Authority" for ἐξουσία |
| LOGOS | "Has the potter no right over the clay" | "Right" for ἐξουσία |
| PNEUMA | "When a potter makes jars out of clay, doesn't he have a right to use the same lump of clay" | "Right" plus expanded paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Both "right" and "authority" are valid for ἐξουσία. Adopted LOGOS's "right" for smoother English. The difference is minor but "right" implies inherent prerogative (fitting for the Creator-creature relationship) while "authority" implies delegated power.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Right" preferred.

---

## 9:22 — "desiring to show" vs. "willing to show" vs. "has the right to show"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "But if the God, willing to show the wrath and to make known the power of him, bore with much patience vessels of wrath having been prepared for destruction" | "Willing" for θέλων; "bore" for ἤνεγκεν |
| LOGOS | "What if God, desiring to show his wrath and to make known his power, has endured with much patience vessels of wrath prepared for destruction" | "Desiring" for θέλων; "endured" for ἤνεγκεν |
| PNEUMA | "even though God has the right to show his anger and his power, he is very patient with those on whom his anger falls, who are destined for destruction" | "Has the right" for θέλων (reinterpretation); "destined" for κατηρτισμένα |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. "Desiring" better captures the deliberative nuance of θέλων than EMET's "willing." PNEUMA's "has the right" changes the meaning from God's disposition to God's authority. Also, PNEUMA's "destined" interprets the passive participle κατηρτισμένα more strongly than warranted.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:24 — "Jews" vs. "Ioudaioi" vs. "among the Jews"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "not only from [the] Ioudaioi but also from [the] nations" | Retains "Ioudaioi" untranslated; "nations" for ἔθνη |
| LOGOS | "not from the Jews only but also from the Gentiles" | Standard English equivalents |
| PNEUMA | "both from among the Jews and from among the Gentiles" | Adds "from among" for clarity |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's retention of "Ioudaioi" is a principled transliteration choice but creates inconsistency since other terms are translated. PNEUMA's "from among" is a minor expansion.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:25 — Hosea quotation: structure and gendered language

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I will call the not-my-people, my people, and the not-beloved, beloved." | Compact compound nouns; no gendered pronoun |
| LOGOS | "Those who were not my people I will call 'my people,' and her who was not beloved I will call 'beloved.'" | Retains feminine "her" (τὴν οὐκ ἠγαπημένην) from the Greek |
| PNEUMA | "Those who were not my people, I will now call my people. And I will love those whom I did not love before." | Drops feminine; adds "now"; changes "call beloved" to "love" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, which preserves the feminine singular "her who was not beloved" reflecting the Hosea context (the personified nation as unfaithful wife). PNEUMA's "I will love those" loses both the feminine and the specific "call" language.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. The feminine singular is significant for the Hosea allusion.

---

## 9:28 — "sentence upon the earth" vs. "word... completing and cutting short"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "For [the] Lord will execute the word of him upon the earth, completing and cutting [it] short." | More literal; retains "word" (λόγον); "completing and cutting short" |
| LOGOS | "for the Lord will carry out his sentence upon the earth fully and without delay." | "Sentence" for λόγον; "fully and without delay" interprets the participial phrases |
| PNEUMA | "For the Lord will carry out his sentence upon the earth quickly and with finality." | "Quickly and with finality" — further interpretive paraphrase |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. EMET's "completing and cutting short" is more literal but obscure. The Isaiah quotation context supports "sentence" as a valid rendering of λόγον (word of judgment). PNEUMA's "quickly and with finality" is close to LOGOS but slightly less precise.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:29 — "Lord of hosts" vs. "Lord of Sabaoth" vs. "Lord of Heaven's Armies"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Lord of Sabaoth" | Retains Hebrew Σαβαώθ untranslated |
| LOGOS | "Lord of hosts" | Traditional English rendering of Σαβαώθ |
| PNEUMA | "Lord of Heaven's Armies" | Dynamic equivalent interpreting the military metaphor |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's "Lord of hosts" as the traditional and widely recognized rendering. EMET's "Sabaoth" preserves the Hebrew but is opaque. PNEUMA's "Heaven's Armies" is interpretive and overly specific.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:30-31 — "Gentiles" / "nations" and "law of righteousness"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "nations, the [ones] not pursuing righteousness, attained righteousness... Israel, pursuing [the] law of righteousness, did not arrive at [the] law." | "Nations"; "law of righteousness"; "arrive at" |
| LOGOS | "Gentiles who did not pursue righteousness have attained it, that is, a righteousness that is by faith; but that Israel who pursued a law that would lead to righteousness did not succeed in reaching that law." | "A law that would lead to righteousness" — explanatory |
| PNEUMA | "Even though the Gentiles were not trying to follow God's standards, they were made right with God... But the people of Israel, who tried so hard to get right with God by keeping the law, never succeeded." | Full paraphrase; "God's standards" for righteousness; "tried so hard" added |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering, which captures Paul's paradoxical statement with clarity. EMET's "law of righteousness" is literal but ambiguous. PNEUMA adds emotional color ("tried so hard") not in the Greek.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS.

---

## 9:33 — "Behold, I am laying" vs. "Behold, I place" vs. "I am placing"

| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold, I place in Sion a stone of stumbling and a rock of offense" | "Place" (present); "Sion" |
| LOGOS | "Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone of stumbling, and a rock of offense" | "Am laying" (present progressive); "Zion" |
| PNEUMA | "I am placing a stone in Jerusalem that makes people stumble, a rock that makes them fall" | "Jerusalem" for Zion; "makes people stumble" — relative clause expansion; drops "Behold" |

**SOPHIA's resolution:** Adopted LOGOS's rendering. PNEUMA's substitution of "Jerusalem" for "Zion" loses the specific theological resonance of the term. Dropping "Behold" (ἰδού) removes the prophetic attention-marker.

**Status:** Resolved in favor of LOGOS. "Zion" and "Behold" are retained.

---

## Summary of Disagreement Patterns

| Pattern | Occurrences | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| EMET's hyper-literal syntax vs. LOGOS's formal English | Nearly every verse | vv.1, 3, 7, 12, 16, 20, 25, 28, 33 |
| PNEUMA's interpretive additions (words not in Greek) | vv.3, 4, 13, 20, 22, 30 | "forever," "if that would save them," "mere," "tried so hard" |
| Proper name transliteration (EMET) vs. anglicization | vv.7, 24, 29, 33 | Isaak/Isaac, Ioudaioi/Jews, Sabaoth/hosts, Sion/Zion |
| PNEUMA replacing specific terms with interpretive equivalents | vv.13, 25, 29, 33 | "rejected" for "hated," "Heaven's Armies" for "hosts," "Jerusalem" for "Zion" |
| Theological terms: different English equivalents for Greek words | vv.14, 16, 21, 22 | ἀδικία: unrighteousness/injustice/unfair; ἐξουσία: authority/right; θέλων: willing/desiring |
| LOGOS and SOPHIA consensus | Most verses | LOGOS's formal-equivalent approach most frequently adopted |

## Overall Pattern Analysis

Romans 9 represents one of Paul's most theologically dense chapters, and the divergences follow a consistent pattern. EMET maintains strict word-order fidelity and retains untranslated Greek/Hebrew terms (Christos, Ioudaioi, Sabaoth, Sion), which preserves scholarly precision but creates opacity for English readers. LOGOS consistently produces the most balanced renderings — formal enough to respect the Greek structures, readable enough for English. PNEUMA frequently adds interpretive material not present in the Greek text (emotional intensifiers, conditional clauses, explanatory expansions) and occasionally substitutes modern equivalents for theologically loaded terms ("Jerusalem" for "Zion," "rejected" for "hated"). SOPHIA resolved the majority of disagreements in favor of LOGOS, drawing on EMET for textual precision in the notes and PNEUMA for pastoral insight in the commentary, while keeping the translation text itself close to the formal-equivalent approach.

---
